6:
"That this may be a sign among you, that when your children ask their fathers in time to come, saying, What mean ye by these stones?" The witnesses note the purpose of the stones, a sign to prompt the children's question — the crux of the memorial that teaches the next generation the works of God. On "that this may be a sign among you, that when your children ask their fathers... What mean ye by these stones?" interpreters expound the purpose. The reformed Calvin and the dominant reading note the teaching memorial — "that this may be a sign among you, that when your children ask their fathers in time to come, saying, What mean ye by these stones?": the stones were set up as a sign that would prompt the children to ask, and so occasion the telling of God's works; the witnesses note the significance — the memorial designed to provoke inquiry and so transmit the knowledge of God's wonders to the generations; the lesson — that the works of God are to be remembered and taught to the children, the memorial keeping the testimony alive; the truth that the great works of God are to be told to the children, "we will not hide them from their children, shewing to the generation to come the praises of the LORD," Psalm 78:4; the figure — the visible memorial that occasions the telling of redemption, like the passover, "when your children shall say unto you, What mean ye by this service?... ye shall say, It is the sacrifice of the LORD's passover," Exodus 12:26-27. The figure of the memorial that teaches the next generation the works of God.

"For the LORD your God dried up the waters of Jordan from before you, until ye were passed over, as the LORD your God did to the Red sea, which he dried up from before us, until we were gone over:" The very brief verse notes the drying of Jordan likened to the Red Sea. The grammatical voices note the parallel. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "the LORD your God dried up the waters of Jordan from before you... as the LORD your God did to the Red sea, which he dried up" — the drying of Jordan was like the drying of the Red Sea, both works of the same God; the for the LORD your God dried up the waters of Jordan from before you, until ye were passed over (the Jordan dried); the as the LORD your God did to the Red sea, which he dried up from before us, until we were gone over (like the Red Sea); the figure (the two great deliverances joined, the Jordan and the Red Sea, the same God; the truth that the crossing of Jordan answered to the crossing of the Red Sea, the deliverance at the entrance as at the exit; the figure of the God who opens the way through the waters at the beginning and the end, "he turned the sea into dry land," Psalm 66:6; the parallel that joins the wilderness journey's start and close; the same wonder at the Red Sea and the Jordan, vv.22). The verse holds together the two dryings joined.

On "For the LORD your God dried up the waters of Jordan from before you, until ye were passed over," the witnesses note the drying. Gill notes the LORD drying up the Jordan (the wonder, Psalm 66:6) — the drying (the Jordan).

On "as the LORD your God did to the Red sea, which he dried up from before us, until we were gone over," the reformed Calvin and grammatical-historical note the parallel: "as the LORD your God did to the Red sea, which he dried up" — the Jordan like the Red Sea, the same God (the two deliverances joined); the figure (the God who opens the way at the beginning and the end); the providence (the parallel, the purpose that all the earth might know to follow, vv.24).

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the parallel — "the LORD your God dried up the waters of Jordan... as the LORD your God did to the Red sea, which he dried up," the parallel (Keil, Gill); "For the LORD your God dried up the waters of Jordan from before you, until ye were passed over," the drying (Psalm 66:6, Gill); "as the LORD your God did to the Red sea, which he dried up from before us, until we were gone over," the parallel (Calvin, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the same God: at both. The verse displays the deliverance: joined. The parallel displays the two great deliverances of the same God. The verse displays the Jordan: like the Red Sea. The verse displays the wonder: repeated. The verse displays the God: the same. The drying of Jordan likened to the Red Sea displays the same wonder at the start and close of the journey: the LORD your God dried up the waters of Jordan from before you... as the LORD your God did to the Red sea (Psalm 66:6) — the drying of Jordan like the drying of the Red Sea, the same God opening the way through the waters at the entrance as at the exit, the two great deliverances joined. The verse displays the purpose, that all the earth might know God's hand and fear him for ever (v.24).
24:
"That all the people of the earth might know the hand of the LORD, that it is mighty: that ye might fear the LORD your God for ever." The witnesses note the twofold purpose of the wonder, that all the earth might know God's hand and that Israel might fear him for ever — the crux of the works of God designed to display his power to the world and to teach his people reverence. On "that all the people of the earth might know the hand of the LORD, that it is mighty: that ye might fear the LORD your God for ever," interpreters expound the purpose. The reformed Calvin and the dominant reading note the twofold end — "that all the people of the earth might know the hand of the LORD, that it is mighty: that ye might fear the LORD your God for ever": the crossing was wrought that the nations might know God's power and that Israel might fear him for ever; the witnesses note the significance — the great works of God designed both to make his power known to all the world and to teach his own people the fear of him; the truth that God works that his name be known in all the earth, "that my name may be declared throughout all the earth," Exodus 9:16; Romans 9:17; the figure — the works of God that publish his glory to the world, "declare his glory among the heathen, his wonders among all people," Psalm 96:3. The figure of the works of God designed to display his power to the world and to teach his people reverence.

On "that all the people of the earth might know the hand of the LORD, that it is mighty," the grammatical-historical Keil and witnesses note the testimony to the nations. Keil and Ellicott note the wonder wrought that all the world might know the might of God's hand, his power published to the nations — the purpose (all the earth might know).

On "that ye might fear the LORD your God for ever," the reformed Calvin and grammatical-historical note the reverence of the people. Calvin and Gill note the wonder wrought also that Israel might fear God for ever, the reverence the fruit of his mighty acts, the end of his works the knowledge and fear of him (the perpetual reverence, Psalm 96:3) — the figure (the missionary purpose of God's works, his power displayed that all might know); the providence (the purpose, the close of the crossing narrative and the conquest to follow, ch.5). The verse displays the twofold purpose, that all the earth might know God's hand and that Israel might fear him for ever.

The witnesses agree the substance: the crossing was wrought that the nations might know God's power and that Israel might fear him for ever (Calvin, Keil, Ellicott, Gill). The verse bears its weight as the crux of the purpose of God's works: that all might know and fear — the works of God that make his power known and teach reverence, the wonder a testimony to the nations of his might and to Israel a ground of fear (Exodus 9:16; Romans 9:17), a figure of the missionary purpose of God's works, his glory published among the heathen (Psalm 96:3), the end of all his mighty acts the knowledge and fear of him. The verse displays the purpose-of-the-works crux. The twofold purpose, that all the earth might know God's hand and that Israel might fear him for ever, displays the crux: that all the people of the earth might know the hand of the LORD, that it is mighty: that ye might fear the LORD your God for ever (Exodus 9:16; Psalm 96:3; Romans 9:17). The verse closes the crossing narrative, before the circumcision and passover at Gilgal (ch.5).
